This 4-week block covers Weeks 1–3 as loading weeks and Week 4 as a structured deload, placing you squarely in the **Build phase** (threshold + marathon-pace work introduced on an aerobic base already established by your recent 60 km/week training).

**Pace targets derived from your data:**
- Easy / Zone 2: ~5:50–6:05/km (10–15% slower than your 5:18/km avg easy pace)
- Threshold (T-pace): ~4:45–4:50/km (approximately your current lactate threshold, ~30s/km faster than easy)
- Marathon Pace (MP): ~4:37/km (3:15 goal)

**Weekly structure:** 5 runs + 2 lifts across 6 active days (1 full rest). Lifting is upper-body-biased on days adjacent to quality runs to honor the 48h lower-body buffer. The two key sessions each week are (1) the long run (Saturday AM) with progressive MP finish segments, and (2) a midweek threshold session (Tuesday AM). One concern: chest/back weekly sets in Week 3 push toward 18-20 with Sat pump included, which is at the upper edge but still within the 12-20 intermediate range.

strengths: Textbook 3:1 accumulation block with a genuine volume-cut deload in Week 4 (compounds 5×→3×, RPE held at 7).
Excellent session specificity: every working set has exercise, sets, reps, and RPE, plus a clear per-session rationale.
Smart leg-day spacing (Tue squat / Fri deadlift) gives 72h between heavy lower-body compounds, and Wed/Sat are correctly de-loaded accessory days.
weaknesses: Week 3 stacks RPE 9 work across Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri with Wed at RPE 8 on DB OHP — the Wed shoulder volume bleeds into Thu's RPE 9 OHP, risking RPE creep on the vertical push primary.
Deload week drops to 4 lifting days (Wed/Sat/Sun rest), technically under-delivering the 6 lifting_days/week constraint that week — defensible but should be flagged to the athlete.

Saturday pump session adds ~3 sets to chest, back, quads, hams, and arms on top of an already full Upper/Lower 4-day; in Week 3 this pushes some muscle groups (chest, back) near the top of the 12-20 set range right before deload.

Split: upper_lower
Narrative: **4-Week Strength-Emphasis Hypertrophy Block — Accumulation Phase**

This plan is built for a 27-year-old intermediate male with strong recovery markers (HRV 58, sleep 7.2h) and a clear goal of gaining muscle with a strength emphasis. The block follows a 3:1 loading-to-deload periodization model (Weeks 1–3 load progressively, Week 4 deloads at −35–40% volume while preserving intensity cues).

**Split:** Upper/Lower 4-day backbone (Mon Upper, Tue Lower, Thu Upper, Fri Lower) + 2 accessory days (Wed and Sat) to reach 6 training days, ramped in gradually. The 6th day (Sat) is introduced as a targeted accessory/lagging muscle session — not an additional heavy compound day — to manage cumulative fatigue.

**Phase:** Accumulation — higher set counts (3–5 working sets), RPE 6–8, rep ranges 6–10 on compounds with heavier 3–6 rep top sets, accessories in the 8–12 range. Progressive overload mechanism for this block: **reps first** (hit the top of the rep range for all sets, then add load next cycle).

**Intensity Distribution:** No running in the athlete's recent 30-day window, so cardio is kept minimal and optional — short Zone 2 recovery work only where it supports blood flow without interfering with recovery between lifting sessions. No quality cardio is programmed; if the athlete wants to add it, it belongs on Wed or Sun and should not exceed 20–30 min.

**Key Programming Decisions:**
- Upper days (Mon/Thu): Horizontal push/pull primary compound, vertical push/pull secondary, arms/shoulders accessories
- Lower days (Tue/Fri): Squat pattern primary on Tue, hip-hinge primary on Fri; single-leg and posterior chain accessories follow
- Wed: Arms/shoulders/upper back accessory focus — lagging muscle work, RPE 7–8, no heavy compounds
- Sat: Full-body pump/accessory session — higher rep ranges, RPE 6–7, active recovery emphasis
- Sun: Full rest

**Deload (Week 4):** Sets reduced by ~35–40%, reps kept at the lower end of each range, weight targets held or marginally reduced. This is a real deload — not just "go lighter." The athlete should feel fresh entering Week 5.
Phase: base
Deload every: 4 weeks
Risks called out by planner: Cumulative lower-body fatigue from Tue+Fri heavy leg sessions — monitor RPE creep on Friday deadlift work; if set 2 feels like RPE 9 when set 1 was RPE 7, reduce load that day | Adding a 6th training day introduces a higher total weekly stress than the athlete's recent 5-day norm — watch HRV trend in Week 2; if HRV drops >10% below 58 baseline for 2+ consecutive days, drop the Saturday session that week | No running base means Zone 2 cardio additions should be very gradual — avoid the temptation to add conditioning work on top of this block without a proper ramp; any cardio added should be capped at 2x20min/week Zone 2 walk/jog

constraint_adherence.reasoning: 3 runs + 3 lifts/week across all 4 weeks, Saturday two-a-day uses run-first sequencing (long run AM, upper-body lift PM) respecting priority and ≥6h gap. Heavy lower (Mon) is always ≥48h from Wed threshold and Sat long run. Deload in Week 4 satisfies the 4-week block requirement. No injury constraints to violate.

periodization.reasoning: Clean 3:1 loading-to-deload, Accumulation phase appropriate (RPE 6→7→8 across W1-W3), deload cuts sets ~35% with intensity preserved (W4 squats 3×6 at W3 load). Run volume progression is conservative (~26→28→30 km, ≤10%/wk). Minor quibble: phase labeled 'base' but Week 1 already includes threshold intervals at 4:20/km — this is build-flavored work in a base label, though defensible 11 weeks out from race.

intensity_distribution.reasoning: Two quality runs/week (Wed threshold, Sat long run counted as volume-quality not intensity), hard/easy alternation holds (Mon lift / Tue easy / Wed quality / Thu upper / Fri rest / Sat long+upper / Sun rest). Easy paces correctly slower than avg (5:50-5:55 vs 5:00 avg). Slight concern: Sat AM long run followed by PM upper lift creates a high-stress Saturday, and Thu upper + Sat upper means two upper sessions with only Fri rest between — acceptable but tight. No Z3 gray-zone bloat.

specificity.reasoning: Every session has exercise×sets×reps×RPE for lifts (e.g., W2 Mon Barbell Back Squat 5×8 RPE7) and distance×pace×zone for runs (e.g., W3 Wed 5×1.5km @ 4:15/km Z4, 90s recovery). Warmups, cooldowns, and per-session rationales are included throughout.

scenario_fit.reasoning: All must_haves met: 3 runs + 3 lifts each week, Wed threshold every week, Sat long run ≥10km in W1-W3 (W4 deload appropriately 7km), heavy lower (Mon) never the day before Wed/Sat quality runs, Sat two-a-day runs first, deload in W4. No must_not_have violations — no squat/DL day before quality run, no back-to-back quality runs, cardio maintained in deload, lifting stays 3x/week.

strengths: Excellent sequencing: Mon lower → Tue easy → Wed threshold gives the 48h buffer the planner explicitly calls out, and Sat AM long run before PM upper-only respects priority-first rule.
Concrete prescriptions throughout — every lift has sets×reps×RPE with progression noted week-over-week (e.g., W2 squat 5×8 vs W1 4×8; W3 threshold pace drops 4:20→4:15).
Real deload: W4 cuts sets ~35% (squat 5×8→3×6, threshold 5×1.5→3×1.5km) while preserving pace and load — textbook execution.
weaknesses: Phase labeled 'base' but Week 1 already runs threshold intervals at 4:20/km — this is build work; mis-labeling could mislead the athlete about where they are in the macrocycle.
Saturday stacks long run AM + upper lift PM every week including deload — cumulative Saturday load is high, and with Thu upper + Sat upper separated only by Fri rest, upper-body recovery is tight.
Long run is 38% of weekly volume in W1 (10/26 km) and ~37% in W2-W3, exceeding the 30% guideline the planner itself cites.

Split: upper_lower
Narrative: **Plan Overview: Hybrid Hypertrophy + 10K Block (Weeks 1–4)**

This 4-week block serves a dual-goal athlete: hypertrophy is the primary focus, with 10K performance as a meaningful secondary target (race: Aug 10, 2026 — 11 weeks out). The plan follows a **3:1 loading-to-deload structure** (Weeks 1–3 load, Week 4 deloads), with lifting phased as **Accumulation (RPE 6–8)** across the loading weeks.

**Run Volume:** Current base is ~24 km/week across 3 runs. We build conservatively at ≤10%/week: Week 1 → ~26 km, Week 2 → ~28 km, Week 3 → ~30 km, Week 4 deload → ~18 km. Long runs are capped at ≤30% of weekly volume, progressing from ~9 km → ~10 km → ~11 km, kept fully easy (Zone 2). Two quality run sessions per week (threshold + intervals) are protected and sequenced away from heavy lower-body lifts (≥48h gap enforced). Avg easy pace is 5:00/km; Zone 2 is targeted at **5:45–6:00/km** (~13–15% slower). Threshold pace targets ~4:15–4:20/km (current 10K goal = 4:12/km, modest cushion for early block).

**Lifting Structure:** 3 sessions/week using an **Upper / Lower / Upper split** in Weeks 1–2, rotating to **Lower / Upper / Upper** in Week 3 to ensure lower-body frequency across the block while maintaining ≥48h between heavy leg days and quality runs. Target 12–16 working sets per major muscle group per week across the 3 sessions, RPE 6–8 (Accumulation phase). Week 4 deload drops sets by ~35–40%.

**Recovery:** HRV 65 and sleep 7h are healthy for a 30yo male — 3:1 block is appropriate. Two full rest days per week are built in. No two quality sessions on consecutive days. Heavy lower-body lifting is placed on Monday/Thursday with quality runs on Wednesday/Saturday, ensuring the ≥48h buffer is respected.

**Weekly Schedule Template:**
- Mon: Lower Body Lift (heavy — squats/RDLs)
- Tue: Easy Zone 2 Run
- Wed: Quality Run (threshold or intervals) — 48h+ after Monday lift
- Thu: Upper Body Lift
- Fri: Upper Body Lift or Rest
- Sat: Long Easy Run
- Sun: Full Rest

⚠️ **Dual-goal caveat:** Expect ~10–20% reduced gains in both strength and running vs. single-focus training. This is the expected hybrid athlete tradeoff. Prioritize session quality over hitting every number exactly — if HRV drops >15% below your 7-day average, downshift (VO2max → threshold, threshold → easy, easy → off).
Phase: base
Deload every: 4 weeks
Risks called out by planner: Heavy lower-body lift on Monday is 48h before Wednesday threshold run — this is the minimum safe buffer; if Monday lifts run long or intensity creeps (RPE > 8), Wednesday quality will be compromised. Keep Monday squats at RPE 7 strictly. | Dual-goal volume (3 lifts + 3 runs/week on 5 training days) requires two-a-days on some weeks. These are justified by the schedule but monitor cumulative fatigue — if sleep drops below 6.5h on back-to-back nights, convert the PM session to mobility/rest. | Run volume ramp from 24 km/week is conservative (≤10%/week) but the athlete's avg run HR of 158 bpm on easy runs suggests current easy pace may be too fast — Zone 2 at 5:45–6:00/km may feel very slow at first. Trust the process; aerobic base improvement depends on keeping easy runs genuinely easy.

constraint_adherence.reasoning: Plan respects 4 days/week (3 runs + 1 lift in Weeks 1-3; 2 runs + 1 lift in deload Week 4), 1 lifting day on Friday as specified, race date is far enough out for base phase, and no injury constraints to violate. Easy paces (8:30/km) align with athlete's current 7.5 min/km easy pace.

periodization.reasoning: Clean 3:1 block (Weeks 1-3 loading, Week 4 deload with ~35% volume cut, intensity preserved via Zone 2 pace and short lift). Week 1→2 long run 4.5→5km (+11%, borderline but acceptable), Week 2→3 5→5.5km (+10%). Weekly volume progressions are within the 10% rule. Base phase appropriate for a beginner 8+ months from race.

intensity_distribution.reasoning: Weeks 1, 2, 4 are 100% Zone 2 — excellent. Week 3 introduces a single gentle fartlek (4×60s pick-ups = only 4 min of work in a ~78 min running week, well under 20%). However, the fartlek pick-ups are labeled Z3 at 6:30/km, which is technically gray-zone rather than truly polarized; for a beginner, strides (8-10s neuromuscular) would have been a cleaner choice. Hard/easy alternation is respected (Tue quality → Wed rest → Thu easy).

specificity.reasoning: Every session has duration/distance, pace, HR zone, and rationale. Lift sessions specify exercise×sets×reps×load×RPE (e.g. Week 2 Fri: Goblet Squat 4×12 @ 8kg RPE6). Fartlek in Week 3 Tue is fully detailed with warmup, 4× work/recovery structure, and cooldown.

scenario_fit.reasoning: All must_haves met: ≥80% easy (effectively ~95%+), max one quality session (only Week 3 has one), gradual volume increases, rest days on non-run days, conservative long-run progression (4.5→5→5.5→3.5 km deload). No must_not_have violations: no VO2max intervals (fartlek is mild Z3 pick-ups), never >2 quality sessions, runs are 3 days/week (not 4+), lifting is RPE 6-7 with light loads and placed 48h from long run, and rest days are abundant.

strengths: Excellent intensity discipline — Weeks 1, 2, 4 are fully Zone 2 and the single Week 3 quality session is appropriately gentle (4×60s pick-ups) for a beginner.
Deload in Week 4 is a real deload: volume cut ~35% (5.5→3.5 km long run, 30→20 min midweek), sets cut from 3-4 to 2 on the lift, intensity preserved.
Strong session specificity with paces, HR zones, RPE, loads, and a one-line rationale per session; Friday PM lift placement correctly protects Saturday long run with ≥48h gap.
weaknesses: Week 3 Tue fartlek pick-ups are prescribed at Z3 (6:30/km) — for a true beginner with high resting run HR, neuromuscular strides (8-10s at faster pace, full recovery) would be lower-risk and avoid gray-zone work.
Week 1→2 long run jump of 4.5→5.0 km is +11%, marginally over the 10% rule (minor quibble given absolute volume is tiny).

No explicit walk-run interval prescription despite the scenario flagging it as appropriate; athlete's avg HR of 165 at 7.5 min/km suggests she may not sustain 28-30 min continuous Zone 2 without walk breaks, though the plan does mention 'run/walk permitted' in Week 2.

scenarios.plan_summary:
Split: full_body
Narrative: **12-Week 5K Plan — Weeks 1–4 (Base Phase)**

This athlete is a 35-year-old beginner with a very low running base (~6 km/week across 2 runs), average HR sitting high at 165 bpm during easy efforts, and sleep averaging 6.8h — close to the caution threshold. Her goal is weight loss with a 5K race on August 15, 2026.

**Phase: Base (Weeks 1–4).** The priority is building aerobic capacity, movement consistency, and habits — NOT speed or intensity. We protect the 10% volume rule as a hard ceiling and resist the urge to ramp aggressively. Quality sessions (one per week) are introduced gently as a short fartlek or light tempo effort only in Week 3 once a rhythm is established.

**Intensity:** Her avg easy pace is 7.5 min/km, so Zone 2 target is ~8.3–8.6 min/km (conversational, nasal-breathable). Long runs are capped at 1.5–2× her avg recent distance (3 km), so 4.5–6 km at easy pace. Weekly volume starts at ~8 km (Week 1) and grows conservatively.

**Strength:** One full-body session per week at RPE 6–7 (accumulation phase), placed on Friday so there is ≥48h before the Saturday long run and ≥48h after any quality midweek run. No heavy lower-body loading — we use goblet squats, lunges, and Romanian deadlifts at moderate load to support running mechanics without impeding recovery.

**Deload:** Week 4 is a planned deload (~35% volume reduction, intensity preserved in short bursts). This honors the 3:1 block structure and sets the athlete up well for the Build phase in Weeks 5–8.

**Weight-loss note:** Caloric deficit should come primarily from nutrition, not excessive training volume. Four manageable sessions per week maximizes adherence, which is the true driver of fat loss over 12 weeks. Grinding through 6-day weeks would erode sleep further and push HRV down — counterproductive for both performance and body composition.
Phase: base
Deload every: 3 weeks
Risks called out by planner: High resting run HR (165 bpm avg at easy pace) suggests she is running too hard currently — Zone 2 pace of 8:20-8:35/km will feel uncomfortably slow at first, but this is correct and essential. Coach her through the discomfort of slowing down. | Sleep at 6.8h is close to the 6.5h red-flag threshold. Any week where sleep dips below 6.5h for 3+ nights, reduce planned running volume by 10-15% and convert any quality session to an easy Zone 2 run. Do not push through. | Very low current base (6 km/wk) means even a modest ramp feels like a lot systemically. Watch for shin soreness, excessive fatigue, or mood dips as early overreaching signals — these warrant an unplanned easy week rather than pushing on.

scenarios.scenario_name: Athlete with knee injury — no impact loading

constraint_adherence.reasoning: All hard constraints respected: 5 training days (Mon-Fri), 3 lifting days (Mon/Wed/Fri), swims anchored Tue/Thu per pool preference. Zero running, zero squats/lunges/leg press/jumps across W1-W3. TKEs and supine straight-leg raises are appropriate knee-safe options. No deadlifts present at all, avoiding the hinge concern entirely. Narrative explicitly calls out saddle height risk.

periodization.reasoning: Sensible 2:1 loading-to-deload structure for a recovering athlete. W1→W2 progression is conservative (+1 rep on compounds), well within 10% rule. W3 deload cuts sets ~35% on strength (e.g. bench 4x9→3x8, pull-up 4x7→3x6) and swim volume from 1600m→1200m while preserving RPE — textbook deload. Running and all loaded knee flexion are completely off the table until physio clearance. Approved cardio: cycling (bike) and swimming. Swims are anchored to Tue/Thu only per pool access.

**Weekly structure:** 3 strength sessions (upper-body emphasis, pain-free lower accessory only) + 2 cardio sessions (swim Tue/Thu rotating with bike on weekends) across 5 training days. Two full rest days each week. No more than 5 training days per week — achieved by placing strength on Mon/Wed/Fri and cardio on Tue + one of Thu/Sat, with one of those days always a rest.

**Injury management:** All lower-body loading is restricted to terminal knee extensions (TKE) and shallow isometric wall sits (≤30° knee angle). No squats, lunges, leg press, step-ups, or plyometrics of any kind. Upper body and core are unrestricted.

**Intensity distribution:** Swim and bike sessions stay in Zone 2 (aerobic base maintenance). No quality cardio sessions are programmed — the athlete's recent data shows 0 weekly runs and no cardio baseline, so introducing threshold/VO2max work in week 1 would violate the 10% rule and risk the healing knee. Split: upper_lower
Narrative: **3-Week Strength-Emphasis Cut Block — Masters Athlete (48yo, Upper/Lower Split)**

This plan runs a **2:1 loading-to-deload structure** (Weeks 1–2 load, Week 3 deloads) in response to the athlete's age, borderline sleep average (6.9h), and HRV of 50. The primary goal is **muscle retention during a caloric deficit**, so the strength phase is biased toward **Intensification** (RPE 8–9, RIR 1–2) rather than high-volume accumulation. Four lifting days per week are protected as the training priority.

**Bike cardio** is programmed as a metabolic/caloric tool — short Zone 2 sessions (20–30 min) slotted on lifting days to avoid stand-alone recovery drain. No quality (above Zone 2) bike work is included; the goal is simply to add aerobic calorie burn without interfering with strength adaptations or recovery.

**Split logic:** Upper/Lower, 4 days/week. Upper A (horizontal push/pull) and Upper B (vertical push/pull) alternate with Lower A (quad-dominant) and Lower B (hip-dominant). This distributes compound stress across the week and respects the 48h lower-body recovery window.

**Progressive overload:** Week 1 → Week 2, RPE nudges from 8 to 8–9 and one working set is added to the main compound lifts. Week 3 (deload) cuts volume by ~35–40%, drops RPE to 6–7, but keeps movement patterns identical to preserve motor skill. Autoregulation override: if sleep drops below 6h OR HRV falls >10% below 7-day average on any given morning, downshift that session one tier (e.g., heavy lower → moderate lower at RPE 6, or skip the bike add-on entirely).
Phase: build
Deload every: 2 weeks
